revision.text:
'''玉勝間'''（たまかつま）は[[江戸時代]]の[[国学者]]、[[本居宣長]]の[[随筆]]。14巻で目録が1巻。[[1795年]]（[[寛政]]7年） - [[1812年]]（[[文化]]9年）の間に、3巻ずつ刊行された。1005段よりなる。
== 概要 == 「たまかつま」の「たま」は接頭語で、は目の細かい竹のかごを表す古語、あるいは竹籠の実と蓋が合うことから、「あへ」「あふ」「しま」「し」にかかる枕詞でもある。宣長が古典研究で得た知識を収録し、有職故実や語源の考証、談話・聞書抄録など多様の分野にわたる学問・思想についての見解を述べたもので、[[1793年]]より起稿し、[[1801年]]までの記事を載せ、その後推敲を重ねて完成したものである。「葬礼婚礼など、ことに田舎には古く面白き事多し」とあり、民俗的視点をもそなえている。宣長の生活・学問への傾注が記述されており、晩年の思想を知る上でも重要な書である。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p610、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1966年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p740、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{lit-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たまかつま}} [[Category:本居宣長]] [[Category:1790年代の書籍]] [[Category:1800年代の書籍]] [[Category:江戸時代の書籍]] [[category:国学]] [[Category:江戸時代の随筆]]

revision.text:
'''玉勝間'''（たまがつま<ref>草稿本の内題に、宣長の自筆で「玉賀都万」とある。{{Cite web|title=公益財団法人鈴屋遺蹟保存会 本居宣長記念館|url=http://www.norinagakinenkan.com/norinaga/kaisetsu/omoshiroi.html|website=www.norinagakinenkan.com|accessdate=2019/03/11|publisher=}}</ref>／たまかつま<ref>広辞苑 岩波書店 1992年</ref>）は[[江戸時代]]の[[国学者]]、[[本居宣長]]の[[随筆]]。14巻で目録が1巻。[[1795年]]（[[寛政]]7年） - [[1812年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]9年）の間に、3巻ずつ刊行された。1005段よりなる。
== 概要 == 「たまがつま」の「たま」は接頭語で、目の細かい竹のかごを表す古語、あるいは竹籠の実と蓋が合うことから、「あへ」「あふ」「しま」「し」にかかる[[枕詞]]でもある。宣長が古典研究で得た知識を収録し、[[有職故実]]や語源の考証、談話・聞書抄録など多様の分野にわたる学問・思想についての見解を述べたもので、[[1793年]]より起稿し、[[1801年]]（[[享和]]元年）までの記事を載せ、その後推敲を重ねて完成したものである。「葬礼婚礼など、ことに田舎には古く面白き事多し」とあり、民俗的視点をもそなえている。宣長の生活・学問への傾注が記述されており、晩年の思想を知る上でも重要な書である。
20世紀以降の活字による翻刻版として、本居宣長全集のほかに岩波文庫と[[日本思想大系]]とがあり、日本思想大系は注釈もついている。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p610、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1966年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p740、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{lit-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たまかつま}} [[Category:本居宣長]] [[Category:1790年代の書籍]] [[Category:1800年代の書籍]] [[Category:江戸時代の書籍]] [[category:国学]] [[Category:江戸時代の随筆]]

== 概要 == 「たまがつま」の「たま」は接頭語で、目の細かい竹のかごを表す古語、あるいは竹籠の実と蓋が合うことから、「あへ」「あふ」「しま」「し」にかかる[[枕詞]]でもある。宣長が古典研究で得た知識を収録し、[[有職故実]]や語源の考証、談話・聞書抄録など多様の分野にわたる学問・思想についての見解を述べたもので、[[1793年]]より起稿し、[[1801年]]（[[享和]]元年）までの記事を載せ、その後推敲を重ねて完成したものである。「[[冠婚葬祭|葬礼婚礼]]など、ことに田舎には古く面白き事多し」とあり、[[民俗学|民俗的]]視点をもそなえている。宣長の生活・学問への傾注が記述されており、晩年の思想を知る上でも重要な書である。
20世紀以降の活字による翻刻版として、本居宣長全集のほかに[[岩波文庫]]と[[日本思想大系|、日本思想大系]]とがあり日本思想大系は注釈もついている。

'''玉勝間'''（たまがつま{{Refnest|group="注"|草稿本の内題に、宣長の自筆で「玉賀都万」とある<ref>{{Cite web|title=公益財団法人鈴屋遺蹟保存会 本居宣長記念館|url=http://www.norinagakinenkan.com/norinaga/kaisetsu/omoshiroi.html|website=www.norinagakinenkan.com|accessdate=2019/03/11|publisher=}}</ref>。}}／たまかつま<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>）は[[江戸時代]]の[[国学者]]、[[本居宣長]]の[[随筆]]。1005段よりなる。
== 概要 == 14巻で目録が1巻。[[1793年]]（[[寛政]]5年）より起稿し、[[1801年]]（[[享和]]元年）までの記事を載せ、[[推敲]]を重ねて完成した後、[[1795年]]（寛政7年）から[[1812年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]9年）の間に、3巻ずつ刊行された。なお、「たまがつま」の「たま」は[[接頭辞|接頭語]]で、目の細かい[[竹]]の[[籠]]を表す古語、あるいは[[竹細工|竹籠]]の実と蓋が合うことから、「あへ」「あふ」「しま」「し」にかかる[[枕詞]]でもある<ref name="Nikkoku"/>。
宣長が古典研究で得た[[知識]]を収録し、[[有職故実]]や語源の考証、談話・聞書抄録など多様の分野にわたる[[学問]]・[[思想]]についての見解を述べたもので、「[[冠婚葬祭|葬礼婚礼]]など、ことに田舎には古く面白き事多し」とあり、[[民俗学|民俗的]]視点をもそなえている。宣長の生活・学問への傾注が記述されており、晩年の思想を知る上でも重要な書である。
== 注解刊行本 == *松井博信『〈中等國文解釋叢書・第3篇〉玉勝間・鈴屋集抄』立川文明堂、1932年 *小林爲三郎『全譯玉勝間詳解：全』[[大修館書店]]、1934年 *[[村岡典嗣]]校訂『玉勝間』上下、[[岩波書店]]〈[[岩波文庫]]〉、1934年 *平木四三二『玉勝間抄精義』修文館、1942年 *久曾神昇編『正解玉勝間』[[弘文社]]、1944年 *田口庸一『〈文法解明叢書34〉玉勝間・源氏物語玉の小櫛要解』有精堂、1957年 *[[大野晋]]・[[大久保正]]編『本居宣長全集1』[[筑摩書房]]、1968年 *[[吉川幸次郎]]ほか校注『〈[[日本思想大系]]40〉本居宣長』岩波書店、1978年 *神道大系編纂会『[[神道大系]] 論説編25：復古神道』（梅澤伊勢三・高橋美由紀校注）1982年
=== 現代語訳 === *吉田健一訳「玉勝間」『〈日本の古典21〉新井白石・本居宣長』（杉浦明平ほか訳）[[河出書房新社]]、1972年

revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/91376817|玉勝間]]"
revision.text:
"Tamagatsuma" (たまがつま／たまかつま) is an essay by Motoori Norinaga, a scholar of Kokugaku (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 1005 sections.
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a [[:en:Makurakotoba|pillow word]] relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
*
*
== footnote == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== Annotation === <references group="注"></references>
=== source === <references></references>
== References ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 <nowiki> [[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]]</nowiki>

The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a [[:en:Makurakotoba|pillow word]] relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]][[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]][[Category:wik]] [[Category:Draft articles]]{{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]]}}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

"Tamakatsuma" (たまがつま／たまかつま) is an essay by Motoori Norinaga, a scholar of Kokugaku (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 1005 sections.<ref name="Nikkoku" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a [[:en:Makurakotoba|pillow word]] relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
*
*

== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.

[[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]][[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

"Tamakatsuma" (たまがつま／たまかつま) is an essay by Motoori Norinaga, a scholar of Kokugaku (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 15 sections.<ref name="Nikkoku" /><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2024-04-18|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2024-04-18|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojikiden. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

"Tamakatsuma" (たまがつま／たまかつま) is an essay by Motoori Norinaga, a scholar of Kokugaku (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 15 sections.<ref name="Nikkoku" /><ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2024-04-18|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2024-04-18|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojikiden. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

'''玉勝間'''（たまがつま{{Refnest|group="注"|草稿本の内題に、宣長の自筆で「玉賀都万」とある<ref>{{Cite web|title=『玉勝間』って面白い本？ |url=http://www.norinagakinenkan.com/norinaga/kaisetsu/omoshiroi.html|website=公益財団法人鈴屋遺蹟保存会 本居宣長記念館 |accessdate=2024-08-05 |archiveurl= https://web.archive.org/web/20220924214428/http://www.norinagakinenkan.com/norinaga/kaisetsu/omoshiroi.html |archivedate= 2022-09-24 }}</ref>。}}／たまかつま<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>）は[[江戸時代]]の[[国学者]]、[[本居宣長]]の[[随筆]]。1005段よりなる。
== 概要 == 14巻で目録が1巻。[[1793年]]（[[寛政]]5年）より起稿し、[[1801年]]（[[享和]]元年）までの記事を載せ、[[推敲]]を重ねて完成した後、[[1795年]]（寛政7年）から[[1812年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]9年）の間に、3巻ずつ刊行された。なお、「たまがつま」の「たま」は[[接頭辞|接頭語]]で、目の細かい[[竹]]の[[籠]]を表す古語、あるいは[[竹細工|竹籠]]の実と蓋が合うことから、「あへ」「あふ」「しま」「し」にかかる[[枕詞]]でもある<ref name="Nikkoku"/>。
宣長が古典研究で得た[[知識]]を収録し、[[有職故実]]や語源の考証、談話・聞書抄録など多様の分野にわたる[[学問]]・[[思想]]についての見解を述べたもので、「[[冠婚葬祭|葬礼婚礼]]など、ことに田舎には古く面白き事多し」とあり、[[民俗学|民俗的]]視点をもそなえている。宣長の生活・学問への傾注が記述されており、晩年の思想を知る上でも重要な書である。
== 注解刊行本 == *松井博信『〈中等國文解釋叢書・第3篇〉玉勝間・鈴屋集抄』立川文明堂、1932年 *小林爲三郎『全譯玉勝間詳解：全』[[大修館書店]]、1934年 *[[村岡典嗣]]校訂『玉勝間』上下、[[岩波書店]]〈[[岩波文庫]]〉、1934年 *平木四三二『玉勝間抄精義』修文館、1942年 *久曾神昇編『正解玉勝間』[[弘文社]]、1944年 *田口庸一『〈文法解明叢書34〉玉勝間・源氏物語玉の小櫛要解』有精堂、1957年 *[[大野晋]]・[[大久保正]]編『本居宣長全集1』[[筑摩書房]]、1968年 *[[吉川幸次郎]]ほか校注『〈[[日本思想大系]]40〉本居宣長』岩波書店、1978年 *神道大系編纂会『[[神道大系]] 論説編25：復古神道』（梅澤伊勢三・高橋美由紀校注）1982年
=== 現代語訳 === *吉田健一訳「玉勝間」『〈日本の古典21〉新井白石・本居宣長』（杉浦明平ほか訳）[[河出書房新社]]、1972年
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *『角川日本史辞典 第二版』p610、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1966年 *『岩波日本史辞典』p740、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{lit-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たまかつま}} [[Category:本居宣長]] [[Category:1790年代の書籍]] [[Category:1800年代の書籍]] [[Category:江戸時代の書籍]] [[category:国学]] [[Category:江戸時代の随筆]]

{{基礎情報 書籍 | title =『玉勝間』 | image = <!-- 画像（「[[」「]]」や「画像:」「Image:」は不要） --> | image_size = <!-- 画像の幅（「px」は不要） --> | image_caption = <!-- 画像の概要 --> | author = [[本居宣長]] | published = [[1795年]]（[[寛政]]7年）から[[1812年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]9年） | publisher = | genre = [[随筆]] | country = {{JPN}} | language = [[日本語]] | type = [[和装本]] | portal1 = 歴史 | portal2 = 文学 }} '''玉勝間'''（たまがつま{{Efn2|草稿本の内題に、宣長の自筆で「玉賀都万」とある<ref>{{Cite web|title=『玉勝間』って面白い本？ |url=http://www.norinagakinenkan.com/norinaga/kaisetsu/omoshiroi.html|website=公益財団法人鈴屋遺蹟保存会 本居宣長記念館 |accessdate=2024-08-05 |archiveurl= https://web.archive.org/web/20220924214428/http://www.norinagakinenkan.com/norinaga/kaisetsu/omoshiroi.html |archivedate= 2022-09-24 }}</ref>。}}／たまかつま<ref name="Nikkoku">『[[日本国語大辞典]]』精選版、[[小学館]]、2006年</ref>）は、[[江戸時代]]に[[国学|国学者]]の[[本居宣長]]が書いた[[随筆]]。1005段よりなる。
== 概要 == [[File:本居宣長02.jpg|thumb|本書には宣長の生活・学問への傾注が記述されており、晩年の思想を知る上でも重要な書である{{Sfnp|本居宣長記念館|2015|p=106}}。]] 14巻で目録が1巻。[[1793年]]（[[寛政]]5年）より起稿し、[[1801年]]（[[享和]]元年）までの記事を載せ、[[推敲]]を重ねて完成した後、[[1795年]]（寛政7年）から[[1812年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]9年）の間に、3巻ずつ刊行された{{Sfnp|本居宣長記念館|2018|pp=14-19}}。文章はよく推敲された和文である{{Sfnp|本居宣長記念館|2001|pp=50-51|ps=（杉戸清彬 「玉勝間」）}}。書名は「捨てるには惜しい物を籠に集めておく」という意味で命名された{{Sfnp|本居宣長記念館|2015|p=106}}{{Efn2|「たまがつま」の「たま」は[[接頭辞|接頭語]]で、目の細かい[[竹]]の[[籠]]を表す古語、あるいは[[竹細工|竹籠]]の実と蓋が合うことから、「あへ」「あふ」「しま」「し」にかかる[[枕詞]]でもある<ref name="Nikkoku"/>。}}。各巻には[[植物]]に因む巻名と巻頭歌がある{{Sfnp|本居宣長記念館|2001|pp=50-51|ps=（杉戸清彬 「玉勝間」）}}。
== 内容 == 宣長が[[古典学|古典研究]]で得た[[知識]]を収録し、[[有職故実]]や語源の考証、談話・聞書抄録など多様の分野にわたる[[学問]]・[[思想]]についての見解を述べたもので、とりわけ[[契沖]]や[[賀茂真淵]]への言及、自己の学問形成、[[漢意]]への批判などが知られている{{Sfnp|本居宣長記念館|2001|pp=50-51|ps=（杉戸清彬 「玉勝間」）}}。いわゆる近世随筆と呼ばれるジャンルは、個人的な感想を述べる現代の随筆と違って考証や抜書が多く、自身の感想や意見を述べることは少ないが、本書はそれらが読み物としてバランス良く配列してある{{Sfnp|本居宣長記念館|2015|p=106}}。また「[[冠婚葬祭|葬礼婚礼]]など、ことに[[田舎]]には古く面白き事多し」とあるなど、[[民俗学|民俗的]]視点をも備えている。
== 注解刊行本 == *松井博信『玉勝間・鈴屋集抄』立川文明堂〈中等國文解釋叢書・第3篇〉、1932年 *小林爲三郎『全譯玉勝間詳解：全』[[大修館書店]]、1934年 *[[村岡典嗣]]校訂『玉勝間』上下、[[岩波書店]]〈[[岩波文庫]]〉、1934年 *平木四三二『玉勝間抄精義』修文館、1942年 *久曾神昇編『正解玉勝間』[[弘文社]]、1944年 *田口庸一『玉勝間・源氏物語玉の小櫛要解』有精堂〈文法解明叢書34〉、1957年 *[[大野晋]]・[[大久保正]]編『本居宣長全集1』[[筑摩書房]]、1968年 *[[吉川幸次郎]]ほか校注『本居宣長』岩波書店〈[[日本思想大系]]40〉、1978年 *神道大系編纂会『[[神道大系]] 論説編25：復古神道』（梅澤伊勢三・高橋美由紀校注）1982年
=== 現代語訳 === *吉田健一訳「玉勝間」『新井白石・本居宣長』（杉浦明平ほか訳）[[河出書房新社]]〈日本の古典21〉、1972年
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist2}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *{{Cite book|和書|editor=[[本居宣長旧宅#本居宣長記念館|本居宣長記念館]]|date=2001-12|title=本居宣長事典|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4-490-10571-1|ref=harv}} *{{Cite book|和書|editor=鈴屋遺蹟保存会本居宣長記念館|date=2015-01|title=宣長の版本|publisher=本居宣長記念館|ref={{SfnRef|本居宣長記念館|2015}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|editor=鈴屋遺蹟保存会本居宣長記念館|date=2018-03|title=本居宣長年表：(稿)|publisher=本居宣長記念館|ref={{SfnRef|本居宣長記念館|2018}}}}

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

revision.text:
{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[category:dd]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[category:dd]] [[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[Category:dd]][[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:dd]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:dd]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:dd]][[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[Category:dd]][[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:dd]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:dd]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:Unsorted link categories]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:kk]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]
