{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:kk]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}

It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>

* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Ouvrage de l'époque d'Edo]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]][[en:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tamakatsuma-qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:Tamakatsuma]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamakatsuma]][[Category:qq]] [[en:draft:Tamakatsuma]] }}[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Tamakatsuma]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] {{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999 [[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] {{draft categories|[[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] {{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]

It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />

- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Ill|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]
== See Also ==
* {{Ill|Kojiki-den|en}}

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}}
{{complex}}
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kokugaku|4=en}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:. It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[fr:Tamakatsuma]]
== Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" /> [[ja:玉勝間]]

== Sources ==
