<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉勝間]][[simple:Tamakatsuma]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q55530290]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|4=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokugaku|qq=}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion tier 6]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makurakotoba|qq=}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|qq=}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] == Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />

The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makurakotoba|qq=}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|qq=}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] == Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />

[[fr:Tamakatsuma]] [[ja:玉勝間]] [[fr:User:Tamakatsuma]]
<!--all this actually mandates is a completion tier 7 ranking. We are not using the promising draft template here. If I get anywhere near implementing it, I will do so based on the actual completion tiers of this wiki rather than whether I added them when they were on simple english wikipedia or wherever--><!--Tamakatsuma--><!--Tamakatsuma--><!--Tamakatsuma--><!--Tamakatsuma--><!--Tamakatsuma--> [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Publications of Iwanami Shobo]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Essays of the Edo period]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q55530290}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉勝間]][[simple:Tamakatsuma]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q55530290]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|4=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokugaku|qq=|WD=Q855159}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion tier 6]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makurakotoba|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|qq=}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] == Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki-den|qq=}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999
<nowiki></nowiki>
}}

The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makurakotoba|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|qq=}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] == Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />

== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makurakotoba|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|qq=}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] == Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />

It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />

revision.comment: Bot: categorize pages with unclosed brackets
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉勝間]][[simple:Tamakatsuma]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉勝間]][[simple:Tamakatsuma]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉勝間]][[simple:Tamakatsuma]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55530290]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], a scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|4=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokugaku|qq=|WD=Q855159}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion tier 6]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makurakotoba|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|qq=}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as [[:en:The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|discourses]] == Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki-den|qq=}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999
<nowiki></nowiki>
}}

If I get anywhere near implementing it, I will do so based on the actual completion tiers of this wiki rather than whether I added them when they were on simple english wikipedia or wherever--><!--Tamakatsuma--><!--Tamakatsuma--><!--Tamakatsuma--><!--Tamakatsuma--><!--Tamakatsuma--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q55530290}} [[Category:1790s books]] [[Category:1800s books]] [[Category:Edo-period works]] [[Category:Essays of the Edo period]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Kokugaku]] [[Category:Motoori Norinaga]] [[Category:Publications of Iwanami Shobo]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉勝間]][[simple:Tamakatsuma]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉勝間]][[simple:Tamakatsuma]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉勝間]][[simple:Tamakatsuma]]
{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}}, a scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|4=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokugaku|qq=|WD=Q855159}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Comprising 14 volumes, with one volume serving as a catalog, the drafting began in 1793 (the 5th year of the Kansei era) and included entries up until 1801 (the 1st year of the Kyowa era), with revisions continued until completion tier 6]] and excerpts from listening and reading. It mentions that "especially in the countryside, there are many old and interesting customs, such as funerals and weddings," incorporating a folkloristic perspective. The work, detailing Norinaga's dedication to life and learning, is important for understanding his thoughts in his later years.
== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makurakotoba|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|qq=}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as {{ill|The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|en|The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|WD=Q10855444|lt=discourses}} == Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki-den|qq=}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999
<nowiki></nowiki>

== Origins == Tamakatsuma is a significant work by the Japanese scholar Motoori Norinaga, consisting of a collection of essays distributed across fourteen volumes. The writing of these essays commenced in 1792, and they were published in stages from 1795 until several years posthumously following Norinaga's death in 1812. The essays in the collection are structured into fifteen sections, showcasing a style characteristic of the early modern period in Japan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2022-11-29|title=Tamakatsuma {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129174412/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8799|access-date=2024-04-28|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
It was then published in three volumes at a time between 1795 (the 7th year of the Kansei era) and 1812 (the 9th year of the Bunka era). The "tama" in "Tamagatsuma" is a prefix, which in ancient language denotes a fine bamboo basket, or is a {{ill|Makurakotoba|en|4=fr|lt=pillow word|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makurakotoba|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} relating to the fitting of the actual basket and its lid, meaning "to fit" or "to match".<ref name="Nikkoku">"{{ill|Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kokugo Daijiten|qq=}}," Selected Edition, Shogakukan, 2006.</ref>
It records the knowledge Norinaga acquired from his classical studies, and presents his views on various fields of study and thought, including the examination of court and samurai customs, etymology, as well as {{ill|The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|en|The_Four_Ceremonial_Occasions|WD=Q10855444|lt=discourses|1=The Four Ceremonial Occasions}} == Etymology == The title "Tamakatsuma" derives from the Japanese term for a bamboo basket ('katsuma'), symbolizing the method by which Norinaga compiled his thoughts. It is suggested that Norinaga always intended these writings for publication, collecting spontaneous insights over time in this metaphorical basket.<ref name=":0" />
== Contents and Themes == The essays encompass a broad range of topics, reflecting Norinaga's eclectic scholarly interests especially towards the end of his monumental project on the Kojiki-den. The contents of Tamakatsuma include:<ref name=":0" />
- Ancient Texts: Discussions and quotations from ancient records.<ref name=":0" />
- Linguistic Studies: Analysis of vulgar words and regional dialects.<ref name=":0" />
- Geographical Documentation: Gazetteers and detailed investigations into shrines.<ref name=":0" />
- Autobiographical Elements: A profile of Norinaga himself.<ref name=":0" />
- Cultural Criticism: Critical views on Chinese philosophical and cultural influences.<ref name=":0" />
- Scholarship: General insights into Norinaga’s broader scholarly pursuits.<ref name=":0" />
== Publications == The essays were released in five increments, with three volumes each, indicating a methodical approach to dissemination over nearly two decades. This method of publication not only kept Norinaga’s work in scholarly discussion but also allowed his ideas to evolve publicly as they were being formulated.<ref name=":0" />
== Legacy == Tamakatsuma is preserved as a prime example of scholarly work from its era and remains an essential resource for understanding both the personal and intellectual context of Motoori Norinaga's life. It is featured prominently in Volume One of the *Motoori Norinaga Zenshū* (published by Chikuma Shobō, 1968) and in the volume dedicated to Moto'ori Norinaga in the *Nihon Shisō Taikei* (Iwanami Shoten, 1978), ensuring its accessibility to future generations of scholars and enthusiasts of Japanese history and philosophy.<ref name=":0" />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki-den|qq=}}
== References == <references></references>
== Sources ==
* "Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary" (角川第二版日本史辞典) p610, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, Kadokawa Shoten, 1966 * "Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary" (岩波日本史辞典) p740, supervised by Keiji Nagahara, Iwanami Shoten, 1999
<nowiki></nowiki>

{{Nihongo|2=玉勝間|3=Tamakatsuma}} is a collection of essays by {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}, a scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|4=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokugaku|qq=|WD=Q855159|1=Kokugaku}} (National Learning) during the Edo period, consisting of 14 sections.<ref name=":0" />
